We're here for you, from questions about your fertility to trying to conceive, to navigating postpartum recovery. We provide expert care for your body and mind, without the pressure to pursue procedures or timelines that aren't right for you.

modules.title: Where you can get dedicated care

children.text: Where are you on your fertility journey?
The journey from fertility to postpartum can feel overwhelming. At Tia, we make sure you don’t have to navigate it alone.
Thinking About the Future

Whether you’re planning for the future, curious about your fertility, or actively trying to conceive, we’re here to help you understand your body and what influences it. At Tia, you’ll find a judgment-free space to talk through all the factors that can impact fertility, from hormones and cycle tracking to medications, supplements, and lifestyle. Our goal is to empower you with knowledge and personalized guidance.
Newly Pregnant

We provide primary care during your pregnancy and help with common symptoms like nausea. Our team can confirm your pregnancy and offer comprehensive all-options counseling so you feel empowered to make the best decision for your body and your life. We'll then connect you with an OB or midwife for prenatal care and delivery.
Postpartum and Beyond?

Having a baby brings big physical, mental, and emotional changes. It’s no wonder many new moms feel a sudden drop in care after birth. At Tia, we focus on long-term postpartum support that helps you recover, regain energy, and feel like yourself again. Our integrated care continues well after you’ve been cleared by your OB, helping you manage fatigue, anxiety, and the changes in your body.
Do These Symptoms Sound Familiar?
Fertility & Trying to Conceive
Struggling to understand your fertility, even if you aren't trying to conceive yet
Irregular or painful periods
Questions about why you aren't getting pregnant as quickly as you expected
Curious about fertility testing, cycle tracking, and fertility supplements
Postpartum & Beyond
You've been "cleared" by your OB, but you still don't feel like yourself
Fatigue, anxiety, mood swings, or a body that feels "off"
Pelvic pain, pain with sex, or changes in libido
Wondering if you're experiencing postpartum depression
Hair loss, changes in skin, or irregular periods after giving birth
Why care for Fertility & Postpartum Support?
Did you know?
Infertility is a disease of the male or female reproductive system defined by the failure to achieve a pregnancy after 12 months or more of regular unprotected sexual intercourse.
Estimates suggest that approximately one in every six people of reproductive age worldwide experience infertility in their lifetime.

In the male reproductive system, infertility is most commonly caused by problems in the ejection of semen (1), absence or low levels of sperm, or abnormal shape (morphology) and movement (motility) of the sperm.
In the female reproductive system, infertility may be caused by a range of abnormalities of the ovaries, uterus, fallopian tubes, and the endocrine system, among others.
Infertility can be primary or secondary. Primary infertility is when a pregnancy has never been achieved by a person, and secondary infertility is when at least one prior pregnancy has been achieved.

Fertility care encompasses the prevention, diagnosis and treatment of infertility. Equal and equitable access to fertility care remains a challenge in most countries; particularly in low and middle-income countries.

"Your health journey should never be a lonely one. For too long, women have been pressured into fertility decisions or dismissed with a casual "you're fine" after childbirth when they know they're not. That stops here. We're building a new standard of care, one where you are heard, you are trusted, and you are treated as a whole person. This is a partnership, and you're in the driver's seat."
Courtney Domingo, NP
What is Tia's approach to your fertility journey?

At Tia, we believe your health is interconnected and your care should be, too. We don't just focus on a single body part. We zoom out to understand the whole picture of your health—physical, mental, and emotional, especially during major transitions of fertility through postpartum.
We Listen & Partner With You

We take the time to understand your full story and connect the dots between all aspects of your health. You’ll work with a team of experts trained in both modern and holistic modalities who listen deeply and tailor care to your needs. Together, we explore your cycle patterns, fertility goals, pregnancy history, postpartum recovery, and everything in between.
Evidence-Based Care

Our care is grounded in science, not trends or pressure. Whether it’s support for conception or recovery after birth, we focus on treatments and guidance proven to help you feel well and stay well. Every recommendation comes from a commitment to comprehensive, preventive care.
Long-Term Support

We’re not here for a one-time visit. Our model is designed for ongoing partnership as your health needs and goals evolve from preconception through pregnancy, postpartum, and beyond. We’ll be with you at every stage, helping you navigate changes and maintain long-term well-being.
Book an appointment
Every stage of fertility, pregnancy, and recovery deserves thoughtful, continuous care. Connect with Tia to get the support you need to feel balanced, confident, and well through it all.

children.text: Fertility Testing and Assessment

items: A judgment-free space for a candid conversation about your fertility
Empower yourself with evidence-based knowledge
A personalized roadmap for your future

children.text: Takes 3–5 minutes to create an account • No membership fee to book • Insurance accepted
Already a Tia member? Book here
What is this appointment?
A No-Pressure Conversation
This is a confidential, judgment-free space for a candid conversation about your reproductive health and goals.
A Holistic Approach
We take a comprehensive look at the many factors that affect fertility, including your medical and family history, as well as cultural and emotional factors.
A Personalized Plan
We treat you as a partner in your health journey, providing a clear, personalized roadmap that is centered on your unique goals.
Who should have a Fertility Assessment?
This appointment is for anyone who wants to be proactive about their reproductive health and get evidence-based answers to their questions. You may want to consider a Fertility Assessment if you are:
Thinking about trying to conceive in the future.
Curious about your fertility status, but not actively trying.
Considering egg freezing or solo pregnancy via insemination.
An LGBTQ+ patient interested in pregnancy.
Interested in discussing how a chronic condition like PCOS, a thyroid disorder, or diabetes may impact your fertility.
Symptoms we care for
We don’t just care for symptoms of infertility; we also care for underlying conditions and questions that may impact your ability to conceive. We can help with:
Irregular, painful, or heavy periods
Chronic medical conditions like PCOS, thyroid disorders, or diabetes
A history of three or more miscarriages
Concerns about a family history of infertility or reproductive conditions
Questions about how age may affect your fertility
How the Fertility Assessment appointment can help

The primary outcome of this appointment is clarity and peace of mind. During your visit, your provider will help you understand your reproductive health through a truly holistic lens. Together, you will create a personalized care plan that may include:
Evidence-based education on what's fact versus fiction when it comes to fertility.
Recommendations for optimizing your fertility through lifestyle, diet, or supplements.
Referrals for lab work, imaging, or genetic carrier screening.
A clear understanding of next steps, whether that's to start trying or to continue your proactive journey.
Your Fertility Assessment: What to Expect
Preparing for Your Appointment

This is a no-pressure appointment, so there's nothing you need to prepare ahead of time. You may consider reflecting on your personal and family medical history and personal goals. It can also be helpful to write down any questions you have, as no question is off-limits.
During Your Appointment

Your visit will be a conversation with an expert women’s health provider who genuinely listens and treats you as a whole person, not just a set of reproductive organs. We'll take a deep dive into your medical, family, and reproductive history to build a complete picture of your health. During this appointment, we'll answer your questions and provide personalized, evidence-based education to help you feel empowered and informed about your fertility.
After Your Appointment

You will leave with a customized care plan, centered on your unique goals and may include next steps like a recommendation for specific lab work, imaging, or a referral to a reproductive endocrinologist. Most importantly, you will have the knowledge you need to feel confident in your journey, whether you're trying to conceive soon or years from now.
Insurance and Pricing
Is this covered by insurance?
Yes! Tia will bill your insurance. Just like at any doctor’s office, you may be responsible for a co-pay or coinsurance, depending on your health plan.
Our experts in Fertility
Preventative health, primary care, chronic disease management

children.text: Rebecca is a DNP-trained family nurse practitioner who utilizes an individualized, holistic approach while implementing evidence-based practice to achieve the best possible health outcomes for her patients. Throughout her career, she's worked with patients across the lifespan from a variety of socioeconomic and cultural backgrounds. Passionate about empowering women through healthcare, she provides judgment-free counseling & information that fosters shared decision-making, so each woman has a care plan in place that fits her individual needs.

meta.metaTitle: Rebecca Reilly, DNP, FNP-C

children.text: Holistic Reproductive Wellness, Abortion, Menopause

Sarah is a board-certified Nurse Midwife with over a decade of experience specializing in integrative, holistic, trauma-informed care for women across all life stages. With deep expertise in reproductive health from menses through menopause, she is passionate about informed consent and shared decision-making, ensuring every client feels empowered and supported in their wellness journey. Sarah’s practice is rooted in relationship-based, high-quality care, creating a safe and collaborative space where clients can access personalized support tailored to their unique needs.

meta.metaTitle: Sarah Stetina, NP | Telehealth Nurse Practitioner | Tia Virtual Clinic

children.text: Herbal medicine, contraception counseling, vaginal infections

modules.title: READ MORE IN OUR BLOG, THE TIA TAKE

children.text: Shayna is a Certified Nurse Midwife who works to support women’s health autonomy and better patient outcomes with an honest, empathetic communication style and an individualized, holistic approach to patient-centered care. She offers space for patients to comfortably discuss their unique health needs free from judgment, with the goal of helping them feel their very best. Shayna has worked with low-income patients of all backgrounds, and is constantly learning and listening in order to better help enhance women’s lives through compassionate healthcare.

meta.metaTitle: Shayna Beumer, CNM
meta.title: Shayna Beumer, CNM

children.text: Preventative care/ Hormonal Health /PMS/PCOS/ Peri-Menopause

Sheila Navidi is a Board Certified Family Nurse Practitioner with over 19 years of experience in Integrative Women's Healthcare. She is a Certified Menopause Specialist and has additional training and certifications in Functional and Botanical Medicine. Sheila believes in treating patients with a holistic approach to achieve optimal health and wellness. She has developed a passion over her years of practice with providing Integrative treatments for women's health issues including thyroid / autoimmune conditions, hormonal health and balance, including care for PMS, PCOS, and Peri/Menopause.

meta.metaTitle: Sheila Navidi, FNP | San Francisco, Nurse Practitioner

children.text: Chronic Conditions/Disease Prevention, Contraception/Family Planning, Peri/Menopausal Transitions

Dr. Reddy is a primary care physician with over 5 years of experience in Family Medicine and Women's Health. She has experience working with patients of all ages and backgrounds with a vast array of medical concerns and conditions. She strives to provide comprehensive, compassionate, and evidence-based medical care while promoting wellness, healthy lifestyles, and disease prevention.

meta.metaTitle: Shreya Reddy, MD | Tia Clinic

children.text: all things women's health, prenatal nutrition, postpartum care including lactation education, urinary incontinence

As a board-certified physician assistant working exclusively in women's health since 2016, Tina have gained extensive experience in both private practice and federally qualified health centers. Her dedication to providing exceptional patient care extends to all individuals, especially those in underserved communities, where she advocates for equitable access to healthcare and reproductive justice. With a commitment to lifelong learning, Tina earned certifications as a lactation education specialist and a prenatal nutrition specialist, enriching my ability to deliver personalized, comprehensive care. Her approach emphasizes trauma-informed care, with a focus on preventative healthcare and patient engagement. She firmly believes in empowering individuals to make informed decisions about their health, acknowledging them as the experts on their own bodies.

meta.metaTitle: Tina Brenner, PA | Tia Pasadena | Physician Assistant

children.text: Abnormal uterine bleeding, contraception/family planning, lifestyle modifications during pre/peri/postpartum

Pegah Tizabgar’s practice has focused on women’s health since 2017. While initially specializing in Family Medicine, she later diversified her expertise through cross-training in OBGYN/Women's Health. Through her experiences in Family Medicine and OB/GYN, it became abundantly clear that she has a deep interest in the diversity of Women's Health as a whole. Witnessing how intricate and complex the female body can be, Pegah’s excitement and interest in this area of healthcare only increased over time. She eagerly anticipates the opportunity to provide care and education to women regarding their unique health needs, employing a collaborative decision-making approach.

meta.metaTitle: Pegah Poulder, PA-C

children.text: Contraception, PCOS, PrEP, abortion

Allison is a board-certified Nurse Practitioner specializing in sexual and reproductive health. Passionate about reproductive justice and cultivating nonjudgemental medical spaces for all bodies, Allison uses active listening, empathy, sex-positivity, and humor to establish and maintain relationships with Tia members. They have extensive experience working in marginalized communities (e.g., those living with mental illness/ substance use disorders, abuse survivors, LGBTQ+ individuals, immigrants and their first generation family members) and are particularly excited to use Tia's innovative virtual platform to increase access in these spaces.

children.text: Women’s Health Clinic in Los Angeles

Why go to multiple doctors’ offices—primary care clinic here, gynecologist there—when you can have everything you need at one women’s health clinic in Los Angeles? At Tia, we offer comprehensive, holistic support from compassionate primary care providers, gynecologists, mental health specialists, and wellness practitioners who are here for your whole health.
Find a women’s clinic in Los Angeles for women's healthcare services

With locations in the Valley (Studio City, Pasadena), Culver City, Playa Vista), Silver Lake, and West Hollywood, we’re making it easier for women across Los Angeles to access personalized care that prioritizes your health. Whether you prefer in-person visits or virtual appointments, our team works together to support your mental and physical health—across your whole life.
Yes, we take insurance
Tia may not look like a typical doctor’s office, but you can still use your insurance for high-quality care—most members do!
We accept most major PPOs
Copays and deductibles still apply, just like any other provider
Your membership fee gets you top-quality, fast, easy access to expert providers, avoiding long waits
Los Angeles loves Tia

“Before coming to Tia, I was in constant pain. With my Tia providers, I developed a holistic plan—including low impact yoga, medicine adjustments, gut education, and mental health that has relieved my chronic pain and inflammation!”
—Alekza, Tia Member
“Tia feels revolutionary because it is. To have medical care where you and your well-being are actually prioritized feels like a breath of fresh air. So thankful for Tia.”
—Nicole, Tia Member

“From the moment I stepped into the clinic until the moment I left, everyone was unbelievably kind and helpful. The check-in process was so smooth, the waiting room was a dream, and my medical assistant and Nurse Practitioners were beyond incredible.”
—Molly, Tia Member

“Joining Tia last year was the best decision I've made for my physical and mental well-being in a while. They've got you covered from GYN visits, to acupuncture (my personal fave), to therapy as well as community events. It's a network of professional practitioners that's enriched my life in countless ways.”
—Dominika, Tia Member
“I am obsessed with the Tia Clinic. At each appointment, I feel cared for, listened to, and empowered with knowledge.”
—Lauren, Tia Member

Tia was created to be a one-stop-shop for all of your physical, mental, and emotional health needs. We also recognize that sometimes, women need specialty care that falls outside of the scope of our Care Teams. That’s why we’ve teamed up with Cedars-Sinai to enable all Tia members in Los Angeles seamless access to premier specialists and hospitals when needed.

children.text: About Tia San Francisco

Located conveniently at the intersection of Mission and Van Ness Ave. Designed to make the female healthcare experience more welcoming and comforting, Tia members can enjoy an open layout waiting room with specialty beverages and new books to browse through.

cells: Where to find us
Our address is 1500 Mission Street. Enter the front door of the building on Mission St, below the Tia sign. Ring the doorbell to your left.
Where to park

SF Mission offers parking validation for the parking garage at 90 11th Street, San Francisco CA. The price starts at $10/hour but should not exceed $20 total with parking validation from the clinic. If garage has sign stating full, please disregard and proceed to park there as we have reserved spot for Tia patients.
When to arrive to the clinic before your appointment

Please arrive to your appointment 15 min early, in case you get lost or have trouble with traffic or parking. If you are late to your appointment, we may not be able to care for you and you may be charged a fee.

children.text: Scottsdale women’s clinic: primary care physicians & gynecologists

Our team of experts—including primary care physicians, gynecologists, and acupuncturists—collaborate to support your whole health, from annual exams and acute issues to complicated chronic conditions.

Whether you're seeking wellness guidance, reproductive health support, or mental health services, we make it easy to get the care you need. With flexible options for both in-person visits and virtual appointments, caring for your whole self has never been more convenient.

children.text: No, absolutely not. This appointment is for anyone who is curious about their reproductive health and wants to be proactive. Whether you are thinking about trying in the next few months or years from now, a Fertility Assessment is a perfect way to get evidence-based information and peace of mind about your body and your future.

faqs.question: Is a Fertility Assessment only for people who are actively trying to conceive?

children.text: A Fertility Assessment is an educational appointment for anyone who is not actively trying to conceive but wants to understand their body and fertility status further. A Trying to Conceive (TTC) appointment is for those who are actively trying to get pregnant and need support, a personalized action plan, and guidance on how to increase their chances of conception.

faqs.question: What is the difference between a Fertility Assessment and a Trying to Conceive (TTC) appointment?

children.text: A Fertility Assessment is a comprehensive conversation with a provider—it is not a single test. Based on your unique history and goals, your provider may recommend specific lab tests, such as hormone bloodwork or an ultrasound, to help you better understand your reproductive health.

faqs.question: What is a Fertility Assessment test for?

children.text: No single test can give you a definitive answer on your fertility status. However, a Fertility Assessment can give you valuable insight into factors that may impact your ability to conceive. The goal is to provide you with the knowledge and clarity you need to make informed decisions about your body.

faqs.question: Will a Fertility Assessment tell me if I’m fertile or infertile?

children.text: Fertility and hormones are complex—there is no single test that can definitively determine your fertility. However, we can discuss and order tests to evaluate specific components of your hormonal health. Your provider will educate you on the benefits and limitations of each option, empowering you to make the best choice for your health.

faqs.question: Do you do fertility and hormonal testing?

children.text: Tia does not offer IVF, egg freezing, or other fertility-related procedures, but we are happy to refer you to a trusted partner.

faqs.question: Does Tia do egg freezing or IVF?

children.text: If you’ve been here before, you’re not alone. You’ve just finished having super-hotsex and while you’re lying in bed basking in the afterglow and feeling pretty awesome, your partner suddenly utters those three little words: Where’s the condom?
And just like that, a night of carefree and connected fun turns into a search and recovery mission. Buzz. Instantly. Killed. Okay, you’ve got this.

You sit up, flex your fingers, and go in. A few seconds later, after groping unsuccessfully in the dark for the rubber that went rogue, your panic-stricken mind begins to consider the possibility that your vagina might’ve actually swallowed the condom whole. I mean, seriously, where exactly can it hide in there? But then you strike ‘gold’. When it comes out, you feel the same way the condom looks: empty and deflated. To make matters worse, you’re pretty sure you’re ovulating and you’re DEFINITELY sure you don’t want to be a mom in forty weeks. So, now what? Take a deep breath and try to relax. Because when plan A fails, there’s always a plan B: emergency contraception.
What is it? Where do I get it? Do I need a prescription? How does it work? Any side effects?

If you’ve got questions, we’ve got you covered. Keep on reading to learn everything you want to know (and some things you don’t even know you want to know) about a special kind of contraception that can really come in handy when your regular form of contraception ‘slips up’ during sex.

editorialText.title: What is emergency contraception?

children.text: Emergency contraception (EC), also known as “the morning-after pill”, is a type of birth control that can be used to prevent pregnancy if you’ve had unprotected vaginal sex anytime in the last five days, if you used your regular method of birth control incorrectly or if your birth control method has failed (condom breaks, birth control skin patch comes off, forgot to take birth control pills two or more days in a row). The “morning-after pill” is a bit of a misnomer since you don’t actually have to take it the morning after for it to work. But there is a short window of time during which EC is effective and, as a general rule, it works best when you use it as soon as possible after having unprotected sex or experiencing a birth control failure.
A few other points are worth mentioning here. EC is intended for backup contraception only and is not meant to be a primary method of birth control. Also, EC is not the same thing as mifepristone (
Mifeprex

) aka the abortion pill. ECdoesn’t end a pregnancy that has started. Instead, EC works by delaying or preventing ovulation (release of the egg from the ovary) or by preventing an egg from being fertilized by a sperm. Oh, and if you’re already pregnant, EC won’t stop or do any harm to your pregnancy.
So far, so good?
There are two types of EC: pills and
intrauterine devices (IUDs)
. Simple, right? Well, not exactly, because there are three kinds of EC pills – ulipristal (ella), levonorgestrel (Plan B One-Step), and
combined birth control pills
– and two types of IUDs.
Need emergency contraception? Join Tia's membership and schedule appointment.
Types of emergency contraception pills
Ulipristal (ella)
This non-hormone medication
works on progesterone to delay or prevent ovulation. It can be taken
up to five days after

unprotected sex without a decrease in efficacy. And when taken as directed, ulipristal is more effective for preventing pregnancy than levonorgestrel (Plan B One-Step) or combined birth control. Ulipristal is available
by prescription only.
Levonorgestrel (Plan B One-Step)
This progestin-only pill
is a synthetic hormone that works in the same way: by delaying or preventing ovulation. It can be taken
up to three days
after unprotected sex, but can be effective if taken within five days. This pill is available
over-the-counter
without a prescription to anyone of any age and can be found at drug stores, grocery store pharmacies, and Planned Parenthood.
Combined birth control pills
These pills contain a combination of estrogen and progestin, and when taken in higher-than-usual amounts, this medication can be used for EC as well. Combined birth control pills should be taken
as soon as possible
after having unprotected sex but can be effective up to five days later. Like progestin-only therapy, birth control pills
require a prescription.
Combined birth control pills are the least effective form of EC and should always be considered a method of last resort.
Types of intrauterine devices (IUDs)
Copper IUD
The most effective form of EC, a
copper IUD
works by making sperm less able to fertilize an egg. When being used as EC, a copper IUD should be inserted
within five days
of unprotected sex. After that, you can rely on your IUD for long-term birth control and have it removed if and when you want to become pregnant. Inserting a copper IUD is an in-office procedure that
requires a medical professional
, usually an OB-GYN.
Levonorgestrel IUD (Mirena)
While copper IUDs have been the gold standard of EC for many years, recent evidence suggests
IUDs that release progestin
are also highly effective. These IUDs also need to be inserted
within five days
of unprotected sex
by a medical professional
to be effective but can serve as long-term birth control for years to come.

One important side note: Being overweight or obese can decrease the effectiveness of EC pills, so if your BMI is 30 or above, a copper or levonorgestrel IUD is likely the safest option since it works equally well in people at any weight.

editorialText.title: Frequently asked questions about emergency contraception

children.text: How effective is emergency contraception?

IUDs are the most effective form of EC and prevent more than 99% of pregnancies when inserted within five days of unprotected sex. IUDs are particularly good if you are looking for a long-term birth control method and if you have a BMI of 30 or above.
After taking Ulipristal (ella), the risk of pregnancy is around 1%.
If you take Levonorgestrel (Plan B One-Step), according to the American Academy of Family Physicians, your chances of becoming pregnant are approximately 4%.
Taking a combination of birth control pills will lower your risk of pregnancy, but this medication is far less effective than other methods.
How soon after unprotected sex do you need to take EC?
Ulipristal (ella) needs to be taken within five days (120 hours) after unprotected sex.
Levonorgestrel (Plan B One-Step) should be taken within three days (72 hours) after unprotected sex but can be effective for up to five days.
Combined birth control pills need to be taken as soon as possible after unprotected sex but can be effective for up to five days.
IUDs should be inserted within five days (120 hours) after unprotected sex.
Can Plan B make your period late?

Maybe. After taking any EC pill, your next period may come sooner or later than normal. Most people will get their periods within a week of the expected date, but it might be heavier, lighter, more, or less painful than normal. You also might experience spotting (light bleeding) during the weeks following administration, which is harmless and will clear up on its own. If you don’t get your period within a week of its expected date, you should definitely take a pregnancy test.
Does Plan B affect fertility?
EC pills delay or prevent ovulation, but they don’t have any impact on fertility.
Are there any side effects to taking EC?

EC pills don’t have any serious side effects, however your next period might be irregular and spotting can occur over the following month. Nausea and vomiting are the most common side effects, but this is more likely to happen with birth control pills rather than ulipristal or levonorgestrel. Other unlikely side effects include: headache, breast tenderness, abdominal pain, dizziness, and fatigue.
IUDs can cause cramping and spotting for a few days following insertion, but these symptoms are usually mild and go away on their own.
Do I need to see a doctor after taking EC?

Normally, you don’t need to contact your provider after taking EC pills. However, if you experience bleeding or spotting that lasts longer than a week or develop severe abdominal pain, it’s a good idea to make an appointment as these can be symptoms of a miscarriage or ectopic pregnancy. Also, you should take a pregnancy test if you haven’t had a period within a week of your expected date.
Can emergency contraception be used multiple times in a month?
Yes. EC pills can be used more than once during a single menstrual cycle, but you shouldn’t rely on EC pills as a long-term birth control method with the exception of IUDs.
Do you need emergency contraception, looking to get on birth control or switch birth controls? Join Tia's membership and schedule an appointment at
the clinic nearest you

editorialText.title: Be prepared, even in an emergency

children.text: So, there it is. Pretty much everything you need to know about EC. To review: If you have unprotected sex, use birth control incorrectly or experience a contraceptive fail, you have around five days to take some form of emergency contraception to prevent ovulation and pregnancy. EC pills are safe and effective, but they aren’t a guarantee and aren’t meant to replace regular birth control methods. IUDs are even more effective (and offer long-term birth control), but they require an in-office procedure for insertion. EC won’t affect fertility, but it might make your next period irregular. And remember: You can still get pregnant if you have sex after taking EC pills, so you’ll still need another form of birth control if you get busy before your next period. Oh, and while EC can prevent pregnancy, it can’t protect you from an STI…so use a condom and make sure your partner knows how to put it on correctly and keep it on. Because nothing kills the mood like an unexpected game of hide and seek with a Trojan in the dark.
If you’re interested in getting on birth control, switching birth control or learning more about your contraception options,
sign up for Tia’s membership
and book an appointment.

children.text: Picture this: you're scrolling through your social media feed, and adorable baby photos seem to be popping up everywhere. Cue the "baby fever" that has many of us imagining tiny toes and sweet giggles. But what if, despite your deepest desires, you find yourself questioning your own fertility? Don't worry, you're not alone.

While fertility plays a vital role in women's lives, it's essential to acknowledge that some individuals may face challenges on their journey to conception. In this article, we aim to provide valuable insights into the signs, causes, and treatment options for infertility in women. We'll unravel the signs, shed light on the causes, and explore the treatment options available to you. Whether you're concerned about your fertility, have already been diagnosed with infertility, or are simply curious to learn more, we've got you covered.

Discussing infertility can feel overwhelming, but it's a conversation worth having. By understanding the signs, causes, and treatment options, you can take charge of your reproductive health and make informed decisions. So, let's embark on this enlightening journey together!
Signs and Symptoms of Infertility in Women

Let's first establish what infertility actually means. Infertility is generally defined as the inability to conceive after a year of regular, unprotected intercourse. While this timeframe is a common benchmark, it's important to remember that every woman's fertility journey is unique. If you have concerns about your fertility, it's always a good idea to consult with a healthcare professional.

When it comes to fertility, knowledge is power. Recognizing the signs that may indicate infertility can help you seek timely medical advice and explore appropriate treatment options. While this is a complex issue, there are some common signs and symptoms to be aware of:
Irregular Menstrual Cycles
: Paying attention to your menstrual cycle is key. If your periods are consistently irregular, either too frequent or infrequent, it could be a sign of an underlying fertility issue.
Painful or Heavy Periods
: While some discomfort during menstruation is normal, severe pain or heavy bleeding may indicate conditions such as endometriosis or uterine fibroids, which can affect fertility.
: While some discomfort during menstruation is normal, severe pain or heavy bleeding may indicate conditions such as endometriosis or uterine fibroids, which can affect fertility.
Absent or Light Menstrual Bleeding
: If your periods are consistently light or nonexistent, it could be a red flag for potential fertility challenges.
: If your periods are consistently light or nonexistent, it could be a red flag for potential fertility challenges.
Hormonal Imbalances
: Hormones play a crucial role in the reproductive process. Symptoms like acne, excessive facial hair, or unexplained weight gain could be indicative of hormonal imbalances that impact fertility.
: Hormones play a crucial role in the reproductive process. Symptoms like acne, excessive facial hair, or unexplained weight gain could be indicative of hormonal imbalances that impact fertility.
Pain or Discomfort During Intercourse

: Experiencing pain or discomfort during sexual intercourse, known as dyspareunia, may be a sign of underlying issues such as endometriosis, pelvic inflammatory disease (PID), or infections that affect fertility.

: Experiencing pain or discomfort during sexual intercourse, known as dyspareunia, may be a sign of underlying issues such as endometriosis, pelvic inflammatory disease (PID), or infections that affect fertility.
Ovulation Issues
: Monitoring your ovulation is important for conception. If you notice irregular or absent ovulation, it could suggest an underlying fertility concern.
: Monitoring your ovulation is important for conception. If you notice irregular or absent ovulation, it could suggest an underlying fertility concern.
Recurrent Miscarriages
: While miscarriages are not uncommon, recurrent miscarriages (three or more consecutive) may indicate an underlying fertility issue that requires medical attention.
: While miscarriages are not uncommon, recurrent miscarriages (three or more consecutive) may indicate an underlying fertility issue that requires medical attention.
Age and Family History

: Age plays a significant role in fertility. If you're over 35 and have been trying to conceive without success, it's worth discussing with a healthcare provider. Additionally, a family history of early menopause or fertility problems may increase your risk.

: Age plays a significant role in fertility. If you're over 35 and have been trying to conceive without success, it's worth discussing with a healthcare provider. Additionally, a family history of early menopause or fertility problems may increase your risk.

If you notice any of these signs or symptoms, it's essential to track and document them. Keeping a record can help you identify patterns and provide valuable information when discussing your concerns with your doctor. Remember, experiencing one or more of these signs doesn't necessarily mean you're infertile, but they can be indicators that prompt further investigation.
Common Causes of Infertility in Women

Infertility can be caused by a variety of factors, ranging from lifestyle choices to genetic predispositions and reproductive conditions. Understanding the underlying causes can help you navigate your fertility journey with greater clarity. So, let's explore the common causes of infertility in women.
Lifestyle Factors that can Contribute to Infertility
Poor nutrition and weight-related issues:
Maintaining a healthy weight is crucial for fertility. Being underweight or overweight can disrupt hormonal balance and interfere with ovulation.
Smoking and excessive alcohol consumption:
Smoking and excessive alcohol consumption can have detrimental effects on fertility. They can disrupt hormone levels, damage reproductive organs, and reduce the chances of successful conception.
High levels of stress:

Chronic stress can impact fertility by interfering with the delicate hormonal balance necessary for conception. It's important to prioritize stress management techniques and find healthy ways to cope with daily pressures.
Genetic and Medical Factors Influencing Fertility
Genetic disorders and chromosomal abnormalities:
Some genetic disorders and chromosomal abnormalities can affect fertility. Genetic testing may be recommended to identify any potential underlying issues.
Polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS):
PCOS is a common hormonal disorder that affects women of reproductive age. It can cause irregular periods, hormonal imbalances, and cysts on the ovaries, often leading to difficulties in conceiving.
Endometriosis:
Endometriosis occurs when the tissue lining the uterus grows outside of it, leading to pelvic pain, inflammation, and potential fertility challenges.
Hormonal Imbalances:

Hormones play a crucial role in regulating the menstrual cycle and supporting healthy ovulation. Imbalances in hormones such as estrogen, progesterone, and follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH) can affect fertility.
Uterine Abnormalities:
Structural abnormalities in the uterus, such as uterine fibroids or polyps, can interfere with implantation or cause difficulties in carrying a pregnancy to term.
Blocked Fallopian Tubes:
Fallopian tube blockages can prevent the sperm from reaching the egg or the fertilized egg from reaching the uterus, leading to infertility.
Pelvic Inflammatory Disease (PID):
Infections like PID can cause scarring or damage to the fallopian tubes and reproductive organs, affecting fertility.
The Impact of Female Anatomy on Fertility

The structure and health of female reproductive organs can also affect fertility. Issues such as uterine fibroids, blocked fallopian tubes, or abnormalities in the shape of the uterus can hinder conception.
Understanding the underlying causes of infertility requires expert evaluation. If you suspect any lifestyle factors, genetic conditions, or reproductive issues may be affecting your fertility,
book an appointment
with Tia today.
How to Treat Infertility in Women - What Are Your Options?

When facing infertility, it's important to remember that there are various treatment options available to help you achieve your dream of starting a family. The most suitable option for you will depend on the underlying cause of infertility, your medical history, and other individual factors. Here are some common treatment options to consider:
Lifestyle Changes and Holistic Approaches to Enhance Fertility
Making certain lifestyle modifications and adopting holistic approaches can have a positive impact on your fertility. Consider the following:
Healthy diet and exercise:

A well-balanced diet rich in nutrients, along with regular exercise, can improve overall reproductive health. Aim for a diet that includes a variety of fruits, vegetables, whole grains, lean proteins, and healthy fats.
Fertility-focused supplements:
Some supplements, such as folic acid, omega-3 fatty acids, and antioxidants, may support reproductive health. However, always consult with your healthcare provider before starting any supplements.
Assisted Reproductive Technologies (ART)

Assisted reproductive technologies (ART) are advanced treatments that can help overcome certain fertility challenges. These options are typically considered when other interventions have not resulted in successful conception. Here are a few commonly used ART procedures:
Intrauterine insemination (IUI):
In IUI, sperm is carefully placed directly into the uterus during ovulation to increase the chances of fertilization.
In IUI, sperm is carefully placed directly into the uterus during ovulation to increase the chances of fertilization.
In vitro fertilization (IVF):
IVF involves the retrieval of eggs from the ovaries, fertilization of eggs with sperm in a laboratory, and then transferring the resulting embryos into the uterus.
IVF involves the retrieval of eggs from the ovaries, fertilization of eggs with sperm in a laboratory, and then transferring the resulting embryos into the uterus.
Egg freezing and embryo banking:

For individuals who may want to preserve their fertility for future use, egg freezing or embryo banking can be viable options. This allows the storage of eggs or embryos to be used at a later time when ready for pregnancy.
The Importance of Personalized Treatment Plans

It's crucial to recognize that fertility treatment is not a one-size-fits-all approach. Each person's situation is unique, and personalized treatment plans are essential for optimizing success. Your healthcare provider will evaluate your medical history, conduct necessary tests, and recommend the most appropriate course of action tailored to your specific needs.
Trust Us, There is a Path Forward!

Understanding the signs, causes, and treatment options for infertility in women is crucial for anyone who may be concerned about their fertility or interested in learning more about this topic. Recognizing the signs of infertility, exploring potential causes, and seeking appropriate treatment options can help individuals make informed decisions and take proactive steps towards achieving their dream of starting a family.

Infertility can be a challenging road, both physically and emotionally. It's important to lean on your support system, whether it's a partner, family, or friends. Consider joining support groups or seeking counseling to navigate the emotional ups and downs of the journey.

Remember, you are not defined by your ability to conceive. You are a strong, resilient individual with a multitude of paths to create a fulfilling life, whether that includes biological children, adoption, or other forms of family-building. Your worth is not determined by your fertility.

No matter where you are on your fertility journey, know that you are not alone. There is a wealth of support, information, and hope waiting for you. Take a deep breath, trust in your own strength, and know that the path to parenthood may have twists and turns, but it's a journey worth embarking on.

children.text: Endometriosis is a common condition that affects many people around the world. How common is it?
Endometriosis impacts
the lives of roughly 10% (190 million) of women during their reproductive years.

Endometriosis is a chronic disease that can cause a constellation of uncomfortable symptoms, including pain during periods, sex, and bowel movements, irregular bleeding, abdominal bloating, nausea, fatigue, anxiety, and depression. It is characterized by tissue similar to the lining of the uterus growing outside of it and implanting on other reproductive organs (fallopian tubes, ovaries, uterine ligaments),
Endometriosis can also
cause infertility

in 30-50% of people suffering from this condition. If you’re living with endometriosis and dreaming about starting or growing a family, you’re probably wondering how endometriosis might affect your pregnancy journey. If so, there’s good news ahead. Even if there is no absolute cure for endometriosis, there are many proven treatments aimed at controlling its symptoms, and many people with the condition become pregnant and have healthy pregnancies and babies.
How Endometriosis Impacts Fertility

Although the medical community has been conducting research and making educated guesses for years, there’s still a lot it doesn’t know about endometriosis, including its exact cause. But there’s something the medical community
does
know about the condition: people with endometriosis often have a more challenging time getting pregnant.
But at Tia, our team of women’s health experts is committed to helping our members manage their endometriosis with a comprehensive approach that ensures their whole health is taken into account.
Struggling with fertility? Become a Tia member and schedule an appointment at a clinic near you!
Located in New York, Scottsdale, San Francisco, and Los Angeles.

For pregnancy to occur, a healthy egg must be released from an ovary and then travel through a fallopian tube to be fertilized by a healthy sperm. Next, the fertilized egg must travel to the uterus and, given the right conditions, attach itself to the wall to start developing.
Endometriosis, can make getting pregnant difficult and impact overall fertility in several ways:
Endometriosis can cause chronic inflammation of the pelvis and pelvic organs, which creates a harsh environment for ovulation, fertilization, and implantation.
Endometriosis can cause scar tissue that blocks the fallopian tubes and prevents an egg and sperm from uniting.
Endometriosis (especially if it exists on the ovaries) can affect the quality of eggs released by the ovaries. It can also damage eggs and sperm after their release.
Endometriosis can alter the environment in the uterus and prevent a fertilized egg from implanting successfully.
Endometriosis can create a hormonal imbalance that isn’t conducive to ovulation, fertilization, and implantation.
Endometriosis can alter the immune system in ways that affect pregnancy.
Endometriosis can damage reproductive organs and prevent them from working properly.
In general, inflammation seems to be at the core of
endometriosis
and the problems it causes. Still, it’s worth repeating:
Many people with mild to moderate endometriosis can still ovulate, conceive, and carry a healthy pregnancy to term.

The medical community also knows that endometriosis is a progressive disease that generally progresses over time, and this is why many healthcare professionals recommend trying to have children at the earliest signs and symptoms of endometriosis.
Endometriosis and Pregnancy

If you have endometriosis and are considering pregnancy, it's a good idea to start with a visit to your healthcare provider. They can offer guidance and treatments tailored to your specific situation, which might include managing endometriosis symptoms (NSAIDs, hormonal birth control methods, GnRH agonists), assessing fertility, and exploring fertility treatments.

In the early stages of endometriosis, you might be able to get pregnant without any medical intervention. Oftentimes, at this stage, controlling symptoms is enough to get past the hurdle of endometriosis.

If you’re finding it more difficult to conceive, your doctor might recommend a laparoscopy to surgically remove endometriosis. During this simple procedure, a medical professional inserts a camera through a small incision in the abdomen to assess your endometriosis lesions and remove as many as possible.
For people who are 35 + or diagnosed with a more advanced form of the condition, your doctor might recommend the fertility medication to help trigger ovulation.

Additionally, your provider may suggest intrauterine insemination (IUI) instead of surgery. During this treatment, when ovulation occurs, your physician will place sperm into the uterus with a thin tube with the goal of improving the chances for fertilization and implantation.
If this treatment is unsuccessful or you have late-stage endometriosis, your doctor will likely recommend assisted reproductive technologies (ART) such as in vitro fertilization (IVF).
There is more uplifting news:
Pregnancy can actually bring relief from endometriosis symptoms for some people.

During pregnancy, menstruation stops, which means the growth and shedding cycle that can make endometriosis worse is put on pause. Many people report a decrease in endometriosis-related pain during their pregnancy, and for some, this relief can continue after giving birth.
Pregancy is still possible with endometriosis

Every person is unique and every journey with endometriosis and pregnancy is personal. While endometriosis can make getting pregnant a challenge for some people, there is hope. Recently, there has been an uptick in attention to chronic conditions like endometriosis and the global toll these conditions can take.

Advances in treatments and a better understanding of endometriosis have made pregnancy a reality for many people who once thought it might be out of reach. Keeping an open dialogue with your doctor, staying up-to-date and informed, and leaning on your support network is paramount. But with patience, preparation, and proper care you, too, can likely navigate these challenges and start or grow your family with success.

If you’re experiencing symptoms of endometriosis and struggling to get a diagnosis and treatment plan, Tia is a good place to start. We take a holistic approach to women’s healthcare and offer a focused care for enodmetriosis to help discuss, diagnose, and manage this notoriously difficult-to-diagnose condition.
Trying to get pregnant with an endo diagnosis? Become a Tia member and schedule an appointment at a clinic near you!
Located in New York, Scottsdale, San Francisco, and Los Angeles.

children.text: Symptoms we can help with
Cramps Or Pelvic Pain

Cramps or pelvic pain can be uncomfortable, disruptive, and hard to ignore. A visit for these symptoms helps uncover what’s going on, whether it’s cycle-related changes, muscle tension, or an underlying condition, and creates a personalized plan to relieve pain and help you feel more in control of your body.
Abnormal Bleeding

From PMS to PCOS, heavy periods to no periods, we can help you evaluate and manage your cycle. Heavy bleeding, clots, and spotting can be symptoms of an underlying issue, and they are never something you have to just "deal with." We'll work together to diagnose what's causing the abnormal bleeding and find a treatment for you. Stop worrying about your cycle and start working toward a healthier, more predictable flow.
Vaginal Infection

From chronic yeast infections to Bacterial Vaginosis (BV) to that "not-sure-what-but-something-is-up" feeling, we've got you covered with diagnosis, treatment, and prevention. We know these symptoms are frustrating, often recurrent, and rarely discussed in a supportive setting. At Tia, we provide expert care to find the root cause, address imbalances, and create a personalized plan to get you back to feeling comfortable and confident, without judgment.

modules.title: Where you can get dedicated care

children.text: Integrated women’s health, all in one place
The Problem

Healthcare should be a relationship, not a series of disconnected transactions. But all too often, it's not. Gynecological and sexual health is too often siloed, forcing you to go to separate providers for your annual exam, a painful sex consult, or a new birth control prescription. Many women feel isolated and alone, passed from one specialist to the next without a central provider who truly knows their entire health story. We know these frustrations are real. In a 2022 report, nearly
one in three women said they felt dismissed by their doctor.

items.signupSubcopy: We need some basic info to find you the right primary gynecologist and book your first visit.

children.text: Takes 3–5 minutes to create an account • No membership fee to book • Insurance accepted
Tia’s Solution

We believe women deserve care that treats the whole person. Our model ensures your gynecology issues are never treated in isolation. Our providers are trained to connect the dots between symptoms like irregular periods or painful sex and your overall physical, mental, and emotional health. This allows us to offer holistic, trauma-informed care that gets to the root cause of conditions like painful sex, irregular bleeding, chronic pelvic pain . These symptoms often connect back to hormones or stress. Our providers don’t just prescribe birth control; we help you find the option that best fits your body, values, and lifestyle.
This approach helps women get comprehensive care, covering all aspects of their well-being:
Integrated Prevention:
After joining Tia, members complete
life-saving screenings at rates 2–3x the national average
, ensuring your gynecologic health (like Pap tests and cervical cancer screenings) is proactively managed.
Seamless Specialty Referrals:
If you need specialty care, Tia partners with top health systems to coordinate your referral while remaining your primary care home.
Mental Health is GYN Health
88.2% of Tia patients were screened for depression
because we recognize the deep connection between chronic pain, stress, and mental health.

Why Gynecological and Sexual Health matters for women
Your body is a connected system.

Gynecological symptoms aren't isolated; they’re often signals from the rest of your body. A dedicated care team helps connect the dots, understanding how stress, sleep, and overall health can influence things like your period or pelvic pain, sexual wellness, or irregular bleeding.
Your symptoms deserve to be taken seriously.

You're not imagining it. For too long, women's pain—from endometriosis to chronic pelvic issues—has been dismissed and minimized. You deserve care from providers trained to understand the full picture of women's reproductive and sexual health who will listen actively and never brush off your concerns
Prevention is the foundation of lifelong health.

Building a relationship with a trusted primary care team means catching problems early, from getting your cervical cancer screening (Pap test) on time to addressing changes in your reproductive health , ensuring you stay healthier for the long term.

"There is no question too personal, too complicated, or too embarrassing when it comes to your gynecologic and sexual health. Our primary role is to be a compassionate, non-judgmental partner in your total well-being."
Rebecca Reilly, DNP, FNP-C
, Market Medical Director
How Tia is different for Sexual and Gynecological health
✓ Hybrid care, built for modern women
Seamlessly transition between virtual consults for birth control and in-person procedures like IUD insertion.
✓ One record, one plan, one team
Your primary care provider is always in sync with your GYN care, so you never have to repeat your history to get a Pap test, STI screen, or tackle a more complex condition.
✓ Seamless Specialty Access
Tia partners with leading health systems to ensure that if you need specialty care—for complex issues like advanced endometriosis—you are referred and supported while Tia remains your home base.
✓ Holistic/comprehensive care
We use both medication and integrative options (like acupuncture or pelvic floor therapy referrals) for chronic conditions like PCOS or painful sex, aiming to get to the root and give you options.
✓ Care that grows with you
We are your health home for every stage—from your first Pap test to navigating menopause and beyond.

children.text: What is a fertility consult?
This visit is intended for people who are currently trying to conceive, planning pregnancy in the next 6 months or have known
risk factors for infertility
. It is a consultation based visit that is available both virtually or in the Tia Clinic.
Did you know?
Percent of US women (AFAB persons) aged 15-49 who have impaired fertility: 13.4% (CDC)
When should you schedule a fertility consultation?

Fertility appointments are usually initiated after one year of regular unprotected intercourse in women under age 35 years and after six months of unprotected intercourse in women aged 35 years and older.
However, a fertility evaluation may be initiated sooner in women with irregular menstrual cycles or known risk factors for infertility, such as
endometriosis
, a history of pelvic inflammatory disease, or reproductive tract malformations.
Did you know?
Conditions that incrase risk for infertility: irregular periods or basent periods, PCOS, endometriosis, history of pelvic inflammatory disease (PID).
What to expect during your appointment

This appointment based visit to review your medical and medication history, pregnancy history, menstrual history and pregnancy plans. This visit does not usually include a physical exam but may include orders for additional testing like blood work and imaging.
Potential treatment plans
Once you have completed your fertility consult, your provider might recommend a variety of different treatment plans depending on the potential diagnosis:
Lifestyle Modifications
Weight management
: Achieving a healthy body weight is important because both obesity and being underweight can affect fertility.
Smoking and alcohol cessation
: Smoking and excessive alcohol consumption can reduce fertility.
Vitamin and Supplement Recommendations
Folic Acid (Folate):
Folic acid is essential for early fetal development. It's often recommended for women who are trying to conceive and during early pregnancy to prevent neural tube defects.
Iron:
Iron is important for overall health, and maintaining adequate iron levels can help support a healthy pregnancy. Some women may require iron supplements if they are deficient.
Omega-3 Fatty Acids:
Omega-3 fatty acids, found in fish oil supplements, may help regulate hormones and improve uterine blood flow.
Vitamin E:
An antioxidant that can help protect egg and sperm cells from oxidative damage.
Melatonin:
Melatonin is involved in regulating the sleep-wake cycle and may have a positive effect on egg quality. It's often recommended for women over 35.
Multivitamins:
Taking a daily prenatal multivitamin with folic acid can help ensure you're getting a well-rounded mix of vitamins and minerals.
Additional Labs, Imaging, or Referrals
In some cases, your Tia provider might recommend additional blood work and ultrasounds depending on the perceived diagnosis. You provider might also refer you to a fertility specialist.
Trying to conceive? Join Tia and schedule your fertility consult appointment.

children.text: Depression Assessment Appointment

items: A compassionate, comprehensive medical assessment for when you feel stuck in a low mood.
Care that connects the dots between persistent sadness, fatigue, and your physical health.
A personalized path forward with a toolkit of support, from medication to acupuncture.
Continuous support with follow-up visits to track your progress on the journey to feeling better.

children.text: Takes 3–5 minutes to create an account • No membership fee to book • Insurance accepted
Already a Tia member? Book here
What is this appointment?
We listen to your whole story

This visit is a dedicated space for a compassionate conversation about what you’re experiencing, from persistent sadness and loss of interest to physical symptoms like fatigue or changes in sleep. Your provider will listen to understand your unique situation and identify potential root causes.
Connecting your mind and body

We know depression isn't just in your head, it lives in your body, too. We'll explore how your mental health may be connected to your hormones, sleep patterns, and energy levels. If needed, targeted lab work can help rule out underlying physical issues that can mimic symptoms of depression.
Creating a personalized care plan

Together, you and your provider will co-create a personalized care plan to help you feel more like yourself again. This is not a one-size-fits-all approach. Your toolkit may include medication management, supplements, acupuncture for nervous system support, or referrals to our trusted therapy partners.
Who should have a Depression Assessment?

This visit is for you if you've been feeling empty, "stuck," or just not like yourself for weeks or months. It's a judgment-free space to explore why you might be feeling this way and find a path forward.
Symptoms we care for
Depression can feel different for everyone. This visit is a space to discuss concerns like:
Persistent Low Mood:
A feeling of sadness, emptiness, or hopelessness that doesn't go away.
Fatigue & Low Energy:
Feeling physically and mentally drained, no matter how much you rest.
Loss of Interest or Pleasure:
Not enjoying activities or hobbies you once loved.
Changes in Sleep:
Difficulty falling asleep, staying asleep (insomnia), or sleeping much more than usual.
Irritability:
Feeling easily frustrated, agitated, or grouchy, which may feel out of character.
How the Depression Assessment appointment can help
This is care that acknowledges the deep connection between your mind and body, offering you more avenues for support.
This assessment is a hopeful first step. It helps you:
Finally get clarity on
why
you might be feeling this way.
Build a personalized plan that addresses both the mental and physical sides of depression or other mental health concerns.
Explore a broader toolkit of support beyond a single prescription.
Feel truly heard and validated by a provider who gets it.
What to Expect
Taking this step can feel like a lot. We want you to feel as comfortable and prepared as possible for your visit. Here’s a look at what your appointment will be like—from getting ready to next steps.
Preparing for Your Assessment
There’s no pressure to have all the answers, but it can be helpful to reflect on a few things. Consider making a few notes about:
Your Symptoms:
What does your low mood feel like? Are there physical feelings too, like fatigue, changes in appetite, or sleep disruptions?
Your Patterns:
When did these feelings start? Do they feel constant, or do they come and go? Have you noticed a link to your menstrual cycle or energy levels?
Your Goals:
What does feeling more like yourself look like for you?
During Your Assessment

Your provider will begin with a compassionate conversation, using guided questions or screening tools to understand the specifics of your experience. We'll explore potential root causes by looking at the connections between your symptoms—like low mood and fatigue—and your hormonal, digestive, and nervous system health. By the end of the visit, you will collaboratively create an initial Care Plan with clear first steps, whether that’s exploring medication, trying acupuncture, or making targeted lifestyle changes.
After Your Assessment

You’ll leave the visit knowing exactly what comes next. Your provider will schedule a follow-up to check in on how you're feeling and fine-tune your plan. Remember, your Care Team is available via the patient portal if questions come up—our goal is to ensure you feel supported between appointments.
Insurance and Pricing
Is this covered by insurance?
Yes! Tia will bill your insurance. Just like at any doctor’s office, you may be responsible for a co-pay or coinsurance, depending on your health plan.

modules.title: Where to get a Depression Assessment appointment

children.text: Absolutely not. Your care plan is built with you, for you. While we offer medication evaluation and management, we also have a wide range of non-medication options like acupuncture, supplements, referrals, and lifestyle support to create a plan that feels right for you.

faqs.question: Do I have to take medication for depression?

children.text: This medical assessment explores depression from a whole-body perspective to create a holistic care plan. This plan may include a referral to a therapist alongside other tools like medication or acupuncture that address the physical side of your mental health

faqs.question: How is this different from a therapy session?

children.text: We offer a range of services to support you. This includes mental health assessments, in-house psychiatry, acupuncture for nervous system regulation, and community support groups. While we don't offer in-house therapy, we can refer you to a trusted partner.

faqs.question: What mental health services does Tia offer?

children.text: You'll see one of our primary care providers (MD, NP, or CNM) who has received additional specialty training in mental health. They are uniquely equipped to connect the dots between your mental and physical well-being.

faqs.question: Who will I see for this appointment?

children.text: That’s great! This assessment can be a powerful complement to the work you’re already doing in therapy. Our providers can collaborate on your care, adding medical and integrative support like medication management from our psychiatry team or acupuncture to your existing mental health toolkit.

faqs.question: What if I already have a therapist?

children.text: Recovery from depression is a journey, not a single visit. This assessment is the start of an ongoing relationship with a provider who will work with you over time, scheduling follow-up visits to see how you're feeling and make adjustments to your plan as you evolve.

faqs.question: Is this a one-time appointment?

children.text: Therapy is powerful, and we deeply believe in it. That’s why we often send referrals to our trusted therapy partners.
Yet, therapy is only one part of comprehensive mental health care. Our broader culture often treats therapy as the default solution, unintentionally overlooking other essential methods of care.

At Tia, we know mental health care for women requires more than therapy alone. Effective care must recognize and address how stress uniquely impacts women's bodies, hormones, and overall health—an insight known clinically as
allostatic load
, or the cumulative impact of stress across multiple body systems.

editorialText.title: Therapy Matters—But It Isn't the Whole Story

children.text: Individual therapy has been transformative for many, and for years we provided it directly at Tia. But therapy alone doesn't fully address every woman's needs. For some, therapy may be inaccessible due to cost, time, or cultural stigma.

Moreover, therapy is not the only way to support women's mental health. Physical symptoms of stress—such as chronic pain, fatigue, hormonal imbalances, digestive issues, and sleep disruption—often require approaches beyond talk therapy. This broader toolkit includes integrative treatments like acupuncture, supplements, medication management, lifestyle interventions, and detailed diagnostic testing.
At Tia, we embrace therapy as a vital part of care, but we intentionally expand the scope of mental health support.

editorialText.title: How Tia’s Mental Health Model Works

children.text: Our model started with a fundamental question:
How does stress actually live inside a woman’s body?
Understanding
allostatic load

—the physical toll chronic stress takes on women’s bodies—informed every aspect of our care approach. At Tia, all primary care providers are trained to proactively screen for depression and anxiety, and many receive additional specialty training in mental health—equipping them to identify root causes and create personalized care plans that address the full spectrum of women’s health.

Your care plan may include medication, supplements, diagnostic lab testing, acupuncture for nervous system regulation, tailored lifestyle adjustments, referrals to trusted therapy partners or psychiatric specialists, and consistent follow-ups to ensure meaningful progress.

editorialText.title: Proven Outcomes

children.text: Our proof points are real: Over 88% of Tia patients are actively screened for mental health versus the national benchmark of 49%, with most of those screenings happening in preventative care visits at no additional cost to you [
2024 Quality Report
The results speak clearly:
73% of Tia patients report significant improvements in their quality of life

editorialText.title: What This Means for You

children.text: We want you to feel seen, heard, and cared for—because mental health isn’t just about checking a box. There isn't one universal solution. The answer isn't always therapy, medication, or acupuncture alone. It’s about starting with you, listening carefully, and understanding your unique needs.
Mental health touches every part of your life. At Tia, we provide care that acknowledges and supports your entire system.
Join Tia and schedule an appointment to begin caring for the
whole you.

main.title: More Than Therapy: Rethinking Mental Health Care for Women

metaInformation.metaDescription: Therapy is powerful—but it’s not the only path to mental wellness. At Tia, we believe women’s mental health requires care that treats the full impact of chronic stress on the body. From allostatic load to hormonal imbalance, our whole-body model includes therapy referrals, lifestyle changes, medication, acupuncture, and advanced diagnostics. With 88% of patients screened for mental health and 73% reporting improvements, our integrative model meets women where they are—with care that actually works.

With 88% of patients screened for mental health and 73% reporting improvements, our integrative model meets women where they are—with care that actually works.

children.text: The next time someone asks - “What’s your gut telling you?” - you might want to listen closely to the answer/to the voice inside your belly.

Why? Because the gut and brain can “talk” to each other through nerves, and there is a growing body of evidence that this bidirectional, back-and-forth communication between your digestive system and your central nervous system can have a major impact on the health of each other.

It might seem weird that your gut has the ability to influence your mental state-of-mind, but if you’ve ever felt “butterflies” or had a “nervous stomach” or gotten a “gut feeling” then you already know there’s a link.

This connection, known as the gut-brain axis, makes some scientific sense too. Research shows there are more nerve cells in your gut than anywhere else in the human body (besides the brain) and more information passes between the brain and digestive system than any other system in the body.
Exploring the Link Between Gut Health and Mental Health

The gut-brain axis refers to the two-way communication network that links the gastrointestinal (GI) system to the brain and central nervous system, and this connection has garnered significant attention in recent years.

Studies have shown that people with depression (and other mental health diagnoses) often have altered gut biomes compared to healthy individuals, and for years, scientists postulated that mental health issues came first and GI problems came next.

But new data and observational research are turning this hypothesis on its head. And now scientists believe that an unhealthy gut can actually contribute to mental health issues and not just be the effect of them.

Research has also demonstrated that probiotics – live microorganisms that can confer health benefits – can have a positive impact on mental health and has been associated with improved cognitive function and reduced depression and anxiety in some people.
The gut-brain connection involves other mechanisms besides the digestive system and nervous system and also includes the immune system, endocrine system, and hormonal pathways.
There are several key players at the heart of this gut-brain relationship:
Enteric Nervous System

The enteric nervous system describes the network of nerves that operate within the GI tract and control digestion. With hundreds of millions of neurons, it’s the most complex ‘nervous system’ outside of the brain, which has led some scientists to refer to it as a ‘second brain’.

This system is part of the autonomic nervous system, which governs the automatic/involuntary functions of your internal organs. This means, while you’re busy doing other things – sleeping, working, playing – the enteric nervous system is continuously gathering and processing information about your GI system and responding without much input from the brain.
The Vagus Nerve

The vagus nerve is a major nerve that runs from the brainstem to the abdomen, and it plays a crucial role in transmitting signals from the enteric nervous system to the brain. This nerve shares sensory information about the gut and receives motor signals from the brain, which allows it to mount a rapid response to chemical changes and the presence of food.
Gut Microbiome

Another key player in the gut-brain connection is the gut microbiome – a complex community of microorganisms, like bacteria, viruses, and fungi, that reside in the digestive tract. These microbes help produce neurotransmitters that convey messages between the gut and brain, and they actively participate in numerous physiological functions, including digestion and immune function.

There is significant overlap between people who have functional gastrointestinal disorders, like IBS (irritable bowel syndrome), and mental health problems, like anxiety, and recent studies show that the gut microbiome likely plays a role in this observation.
Experiencing digestive issues? Join Tia's membership and schedule an appointment to get checked at the clinic nearest you!
Located in New York, Phoenix, San Francisco, and Los Angeles.
How Gut Health Influences Mental Health
The relationship between gut health and mental health is complicated, and researchers are just starting to better understand the connection. Here are a few of the mechanisms involved:
Neurotransmitter Production:

Many of the neurotransmitters critical for mood regulation, such as serotonin, dopamine, and gamma-aminobutyric acid (GABA), are produced in the gut. In fact, approximately 90% of the body's serotonin - a neurotransmitter associated with feelings of well-being and happiness - is produced in the gut.
Inflammation and the Immune System:

Chronic inflammation is increasingly recognized as a contributing factor to various mental health conditions, including depression and anxiety. The gut microbiome helps regulate the body's immune response, and an imbalance in gut bacteria can lead to increased inflammation, impaired brain function, and the development of mental health disorders.
The Gut Barrier and Leaky Gut Syndrome:

The gut lining serves as a barrier that prevents harmful substances, such as toxins and pathogens, from entering the bloodstream. When this barrier is compromised - a condition often referred to as "leaky gut" - it can allow these harmful substances to enter the bloodstream. This process triggers an immune response and inflammation, which can lead to cognitive decline and mental health issues.
Stress Response and the HPA Axis:

The hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis is a central part of the body's stress response system. Research suggests that the gut microbiome can influence the activity of the HPA axis and affect how the body responds to stress. Unhealthy amounts of stress can cause inflammation and impaired immunity which, in turn, can cause mental health problems.
Steps to Support Gut and Mental Health

Considering the strong connection between gut health and mental health, it pays to have a strategy for supporting and improving your brain and belly health. Experts continue to explore the best ways to nurture the gut-brain connection, but for now, their best advice involves two primary considerations: improving your diet and reducing your stress.
Improving Your Diet

A diet rich in fiber, fruit, vegetables, and fermented food can promote a healthier gut biome. Yogurt, kefir, and kimchi contain gut-friendly probiotics while garlic, onions, and bananas have prebiotics that help feed healthy gut bacteria.
Antioxidants and anti-inflammatory foods, which are found in whole foods, like fruits and vegetables, fight harmful free radicals and can help prevent inflammation.
Limiting the intake of processed food, sugar, and alcohol can help too. These substances can disrupt the gut biome and cause an inflammatory response.
Reducing Stress
Chronic stress has a major impact on gut and brain health, so finding effective ways to manage stress is crucial to maintaining a strong gut-brain connection.
Mindfulness practices, like meditation and yoga, deep breathing exercises, regular exercise, and better sleep are all great ways to reduce stress and improve your gut and mental health.

Try therapy. Cognitive behavioral therapy, gut-directed relaxation training, and biofeedback are all wonderful ways to reduce stress and improve gut health, mental health, and overall health and wellbeing.

The gut-brain axis is a super highway of nerves and chemical messengers that connect our digestive system and central nervous system, and this connection underscores the importance of taking a holistic approach to health and wellbeing. While science continues to explore this complex relationship, we can nurture our gut biomes with lifestyle changes, like eating a healthy diet and reducing stress levels, and improve our gut health, brain health, and overall health and wellbeing in the process.
Need gut health support? Join Tia's membership and schedule an appointment to get checked at the clinic nearest you!
Located in New York, Phoenix, San Francisco, and Los Angeles.
